petitioner_background: The petitioner is a TV and Film Development and Publicity Consultant who describes her work as 'publicity and development consultant for TV and film, independent producer/writer.' She previously served as Head of Development at the South Australian Film Corporation and Head of Development at Network 10 in Australia, where she was credited with developing successful programming including 'Heartbreak High.' She sought classification as an alien of extraordinary ability under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act, and at the time of filing (June 17, 2003) was working on U.S. projects including 'The Joy House,' 'Imagine,' and 'Breaking the Walls of Silence: A Journey in Human Fertility.'

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of her association with award-winning projects, but did not submit evidence of awards received personally.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director concluded that while the petitioner had demonstrated her association with award winning projects, she had not demonstrated that she was individually recognized or the recipient of any awards personally. Prior counsel did not challenge that conclusion on appeal, and the AAO concurred with the director's analysis.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of her union and professional memberships in associations in her field.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director concluded that the petitioner's union and professional memberships were not the type of exclusive memberships contemplated by this criterion. Prior counsel did not challenge this conclusion on appeal. The AAO concurred, noting that none of the materials regarding the associations of which the petitioner is a member suggest that they require outstanding achievements of their members as judged by recognized national or international experts in their field.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted published materials, including an article primarily about her that appeared in the Sunday Mail in 1988.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director concluded that the published materials submitted were not primarily about her and did not appear in major trade publications or other major media. The AAO concurred, noting that the only article primarily about the petitioner appears in the Sunday Mail in 1988. The record contains no evidence that this publication has a national distribution. Moreover, a newspaper article from 1988 is not evidence of the petitioner's sustained acclaim as of June 17, 2003, the date the petitioner filed the instant petition.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of her status as a voting member of associations in her field (including the Australian Film Institute), her supervisory duties, and her participation as a judge in the Screen Producers Association of Australia's emerging producer pitching competitions.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director concluded that the petitioner's status as a voting member of associations in her field and her supervisory duties were inherent to her occupation and not indicative of national or international acclaim. The AAO concurred, noting that supervisory duties inherent to a managerial position cannot serve to meet this criterion. The AAO stated that more persuasive than the voting privileges granted to all members of the Australian Film Institute would be selection for the exclusive committee that chooses the award nominees. Regarding the pitching competitions, the record included little information regarding the significance of the competition, how many judges participate and the selection process for judges, so the AAO could not determine whether this participation was indicative of national or international acclaim. Moreover, it was not clear that judging the pitches was judging the work of others in her field of promotion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner relied on reference letters attesting to her development of successful programming as Head of Development at Network 10 in Australia. In response to the director's request for additional evidence, the petitioner submitted a letter from an Australian correspondent in the entertainment industry of 15 years discussing her accomplishments as Head of Development at the South Australian Film Corporation and at Network 10. The letter stated the petitioner's 'aggressive approach in terms of marking the studios, seeking and developing project ideas, offering incentives to the production community and entering into co-venture deals with local and international partners, turned the industry around,' noting that the industry, previously in a major recession, began working at full capacity. The letter explained that prior to the petitioner's arrival, Network 10 had experienced a takeover and the new individuals in charge found no projects in development, and the petitioner aggressively commissioned and developed programs including the groundbreaking 'Heartbreak High' followed by other bold programming decisions that achieved high ratings in both Australia and internationally. Many shows developed by the petitioner were still in production after 10 years. Other letters from members of the entertainment industry provided similar information. The petitioner also cited U.S. projects 'The Joy House,' 'Imagine,' and 'Breaking the Walls of Silence: A Journey in Human Fertility.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director concluded that the letters were not sufficiently supported by evidence corroborating the significance of the petitioner's contributions, noting that most of the references had worked with the petitioner, and that the record did not reflect that the petitioner's work 'has led to changes in the field, or that it has been used as a training tool by others in the field.' On appeal, prior counsel asserted that the director erred in suggesting that references who had worked with the petitioner were biased, and that the letters were from renowned members of the field whose familiarity allowed them to assess her contributions. The AAO found that the record satisfactorily demonstrates the petitioner's influence on Australian television, but the record contained less persuasive evidence of the petitioner's influence after 1996 when she left Network 10. The petitioner's U.S. projects were not complete as of the date of filing and, as such, the AAO could not gauge their impact. The evidence relating to this criterion was therefore not evidence of sustained acclaim as of the date of filing.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of the typical airing of TV programs with which she was affiliated.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director concluded that the typical airing of programs with which the petitioner is affiliated was insufficient to meet this criterion. Prior counsel did not challenge this conclusion on appeal, and the AAO concurred with the director's analysis.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of her roles as Head of Development at the South Australian Film Corporation and Head of Development at Network 10 in Australia.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director concluded that the petitioner meets this criterion and the AAO concurred with that conclusion, although the record contained little evidence relating to this criterion after 1996. The AAO noted that even accepting that the petitioner met this criterion and the contributions criterion despite the lack of recent evidence, the record fell far short of establishing that the petitioner met a third criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Prior counsel asserted that the petitioner was 'one of the highest paid female executives in Australia' while Head of Program Development at Network 10. The petitioner submitted evidence of her gross income in Australian dollars: $83,983 in 1995, $82,663 in 1996, and taxable income of $58,418 in 1994. She also submitted advertisements for comparable positions: AusFilm commissioner in Los Angeles at a salary of $100,000; Chief Executive for Screen West in Australia with a salary range of $91,299 - $98,163; Director of Production Development in Australia with a salary range of $79,687 to $86,553; and Children's Series Producer - Television in Australia at a salary of $100,000. The petitioner also submitted income distribution charts for the entire television services industry and statistical charts purportedly reflecting that she was one of the highest paid female TV executives in Australia.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director concluded that the petitioner had not established that she received a significantly high remuneration in relation to others in the same occupation. On appeal, prior counsel asserted that the director failed to mention the statistical charts. The AAO found that the job advertisements submitted by the petitioner reflected that the petitioner's salary was within the normal range for her occupation. The income distribution charts for the entire television services industry were not useful in evaluating the petitioner's remuneration for her specific occupation, as it was not useful to compare the petitioner's remuneration with those working in menial technical positions in the television industry. The AAO upheld the director's ultimate conclusion that the petitioner had not established that she meets this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Prior counsel asserted that the petitioner met this criterion based on the longevity of the television series she promoted or approved for development, including 'Beyond 2000' and 'Beyond Tomorrow.' Prior counsel further asserted that the petitioner had included in her client roster an award-winning actor and that she was largely responsible for his recent commercial success on 'The West Wing.' The record contained a letter from the actor stating that he had collaborated with the petitioner on promoting charity events, making no mention of contributions to the success of his character on 'West Wing.' Prior counsel also referenced an unsigned letter purportedly from another individual stating: 'I met [the petitioner] in 1999 while consulting with mutual client on the successful Emmy winning show The West Wing for NBC. She worked as a consultant on his behalf and I found her to be extremely helpful and dedicated. Her insight and expertise was immeasurable to the success of the show and Rob's career.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director concluded that other than the reference letters, the record contained no evidence that the petitioner was primarily or largely responsible for the success of the programs with which she was associated. The AAO did not contest the commercial success of several programs with which the petitioner was affiliated, but noted that not every individual associated with a commercially successful project can be credited with the project's success. The petitioner did not submit evidence that she is included in the credits for these projects. While promotion is important for commercial success, the main credit goes to the producers, directors and main cast members. The most persuasive evidence of the petitioner's promotion of a commercially successful project was her work on 'Beyond 2000' and 'Beyond Tomorrow,' but she worked on these projects more than 10 years prior to filing, so that work was not evidence of sustained acclaim as of the date of filing. Regarding the claim that the petitioner was responsible for the actor's recent commercial success, the record contained no evidence that the petitioner was credited with any technical, executive or consulting role for any episode of 'The West Wing,' and did not reveal that the petitioner ever worked as the actor's agent. The letter purportedly from the other individual was unsigned and thus had no evidentiary value, and his assertions were not supported by statements from the actor or the writers, producers or directors of 'The West Wing' explaining the petitioner's role.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal because review of the record did not establish that the petitioner had distinguished herself as a development and publicity consultant to such an extent that she may be said to have achieved sustained national or international acclaim or to be within the small percentage at the very top of her field. The evidence indicated that the petitioner shows talent as a development and publicity consultant, but was not persuasive that her achievements set her significantly above almost all others in her field. Even if the AAO accepted that the petitioner met both the contributions criterion and the leading role criterion despite the lack of recent evidence relating to these criteria, the record fell far short of establishing that the petitioner met a third criterion. The burden of proof remains entirely with the petitioner under Section 291 of the Act, and the petitioner had not sustained that burden.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Prior counsel indicated a supplemental brief would be submitted within 120 days, but before that period expired the petitioner obtained new counsel; CIS received nothing further from prior counsel, and current counsel submitted a brief relating to a separate petition but not the instant petition, so the appeal was adjudicated on the record of proceedings. Additionally, a letter purportedly from a third party submitted in response to the director's request for additional evidence was unsigned and thus had no evidentiary value.
